perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to its conflicts of law principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be generally guided by the principles of Delaware law, with interpretations made at the discretion of the prevailing party's legal counsel.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces ambiguity by replacing 'governed by and construed in accordance with the laws' with 'generally guided by the principles,' making the application of Delaware law less strict. The phrase 'interpretations made at the discretion of the prevailing party's legal counsel' introduces further ambiguity and potential bias. Delaware law requires impartial interpretation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware Code Title 1, § 302 outlines the rules for statutory construction, which require impartial and reasonable interpretation of the law, contradicting the 'discretion of the prevailing party's legal counsel' clause introduced in the modified text. The modification introduces bias and conflicts with the principle of fair and impartial application of Delaware law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 1 Delaware Code § 302 (2023) - Definitions. :: 2023 Delaware Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Delaware Code 2023 Delaware Code (here) 2022 Delaware Code View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2023 Delaware Code Title 1 - General Provisions Chapter 3. INTERPRETATION OF STATUTES § 302. Definitions. Universal Citation: 1 DE Code § 302 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 302. Definitions. In the construction of this Code and of all other statutes of this State, unless the context requires a different meaning: (1) “Adult” or “adult person” means a person of the age of 18 years or older. (2) “Child” means a person who has not reached the age of 18 years. (3) “Full age” means the age of 18 years or older. (4) “Grantee” includes every person to whom a freehold estate or interest is conveyed. (5) “Grantor” includes every person by whom a freehold estate or interest is conveyed. (6) “Infancy” means an age of less than 18 years. (7) “Infant” means a person who has not reached the age of 18 years. (8) “Inhabitant” means a resident in any place. (9) “Kin” and “kindred,” as applied to the descent of estates, signify kin or kindred by blood, and the degrees of consanguinity shall be computed by the civil law method; but collateral kindred claiming through a nearer common ancestor, shall be preferred to those claiming through a more remote common ancestor. (10) “Lawful age” means the age of 18 years or older. (11) “Minor” or “minor child” means a person who has not reached the age of 18 years. (12) “Money” or “dollars” means lawful money of the United States. (13) “Month” means a calendar month, unless otherwise expressed. (14) “Oath” includes affirmation in all cases where an affirmation may be substituted for an oath, and “sworn” includes affirmed; and the forms shall be varied
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates that the agreement is 'governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware,' indicating a strict adherence to Delaware law. The changed text weakens this by stating the agreement will be 'generally guided by the principles of Delaware law, with interpretations made at the discretion of the prevailing party's legal counsel.' This introduces ambiguity and potential bias. Delaware Code Title 1, § 302 defines terms for statutory construction, implying a need for impartial interpretation. The 'discretion of the prevailing party's legal counsel' clause in the changed text contradicts this principle of fair and impartial application of Delaware law, creating a contradiction. Snippet 2, '1 Delaware Code § 302 (2023) - Definitions,' supports this, as definitions are foundational to impartial interpretation, which the changed text undermines.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees to protect the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party with the same degree of care that it uses to protect its own confidential information, but in no event less than a reasonable degree of care.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party will endeavor to protect the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party with care, dependent on available resources and perceived importance.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'agrees to protect' with 'will endeavor to protect' weakens the obligation. The addition of 'dependent on available resources and perceived importance' makes the level of protection subjective and based on the Receiving Party's assessment, which contradicts the baseline of 'a reasonable degree of care' and introduces ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware Uniform Trade Secrets Act (DUTSA), 6 Del. C. § 2001
perturbation.law_explanation: The Delaware Uniform Trade Secrets Act (DUTSA), specifically 6 Del. C. § 2001, implies a reasonable standard of care to protect trade secrets. By making the level of protection dependent on 'available resources and perceived importance', the modified text contradicts the objective standard required by DUTSA, creating an ambiguous and potentially insufficient level of protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 6 Delaware Code § 2001 (2023) - Definitions. :: 2023 Delaware Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Delaware Code 2023 Delaware Code (here) 2022 Delaware Code View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2023 Delaware Code Title 6 - Commerce and Trade Chapter 20. TRADE SECRETS § 2001. Definitions. Universal Citation: 6 DE Code § 2001 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Next § 2001. Definitions. The following words, terms and phrases, when used in this chapter, shall have the meanings ascribed to them in this section, except where the context clearly indicates a different meaning: (1) “Improper means” shall include theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) “Misappropriation” shall mean: a. Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or b. Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade was: A. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; B. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or C. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of the person's position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) “Person” shall mean a natural person, corporation, statutory trust, business trust, estate, trust, partnershi
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces a clear obligation to protect confidential information with a weaker 'endeavor to protect' standard, dependent on 'available resources and perceived importance.' This introduces ambiguity and subjectivity, potentially falling below the 'reasonable degree of care' implicitly required by trade secret law. While the provided snippet defines 'Misappropriation' and 'Improper means' in the context of trade secrets, it doesn't explicitly define 'reasonable care'. However, the Law Explanation suggests that Delaware law (DUTSA) implies such a standard. The original text sets a clear baseline, whereas the changed text allows for a lower standard of protection based on the Receiving Party's assessment, creating a contradiction because it introduces conditions that could lead to insufficient protection under the law, potentially enabling misappropriation through means that might not have occurred under the original language.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior or contemporaneous communications and proposals, whether oral or written.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement generally outlines the understanding between the parties and may be subject to further clarifications as needed.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause is an integration clause that establishes the contract as the complete and final agreement. The modified version weakens this, saying it 'generally outlines the understanding' and 'may be subject to further clarifications,' opening the door to disputes about what the actual agreement is. It contradicts the principle of contractual certainty.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law regarding contract interpretation, generally.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law, following the Restatement (Second) of Contracts, emphasizes the importance of clearly defined contractual obligations and unambiguous contract terms. The modified text, by stating that the agreement 'generally outlines the understanding' and 'may be subject to further clarifications,' undermines this principle, introducing uncertainty and ambiguity into the contract, which are generally disfavored by contract law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contract A contract is an agreement between parties , creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law . The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent , expressed by a valid offer and acceptance ; adequate consideration ; capacity ; and legality . In some states , elements of consideration can be satisfied by a valid substitute. Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance . Background : Contracts are promises that the law will enforce. Contract law is generally governed by state common law , and while general overall contract law is common throughout the country, some specific court interpretations of a particular element of the contract may vary between the states. If a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party, often in the form of monetary damages, or in limited circumstances, in the form of specific performance of the promise made. Elements -- Consideration and Mutual Assent Contracts arise when a duty comes into existence, because of a promise made by one of the parties. To be legally binding as a contract, a promise must be exchanged for adequate consideration. There are two different theories or definitions of consideration: Bargain Theory of Consideration and Benefit-Detriment theory of consideration. Benefit-Detriment Under the b enefit-detriment theory, an adequate consideration exists only when a promise made to the benefit of the promisor or to the detriment of the promisee, which reasonably and fairly induces the promisor to make a promise for something else for the promisee. For example, promises that are purely gifts are not considered enforceable because the personal satisfaction the grantor of the promise may receive from the act of generosity is normally not considered sufficient detriment to constitute adequate consideration. Bargain-for-Exchange Under Bargain-for-Exchange theory of consideration, adequate consideration exists when a promisor makes a promise in return for something else. Here, the essential condition is that the promisor was given something specifically to induce the promise being made. In other words, the bargain for exchange theory is different from the detriment-benefit theory in that the focus in bargain for exchange theory seems to be the parties’ motive for making the promises and the parties’ subjective mutual assent, while in detriment benefit theory, the focus seems to be an objective legal detriment or benefit to the parties. Governing Laws Contracts are mainly governed by state statutory and common (judge-made) law and private law (i.e. the private agreement). Private law principally includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. This private law may override many of the rules otherwise established by state law. Statutory law, such as the Statute of Fraud , may require some kinds of contracts be put in writing and executed with particular formalities, for the contract to be enforceable. Otherwise, the parties may enter into a binding agreement without signing a formal written document. For example, Virginia Supreme Court has held in Lucy v. Zehmer that even an agreement made on a piece of napkin can be considered a valid contract, if the parties were both sane, and showed mutual assent and consideration. Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute. The Uniform Commercial Code , whose original articles have been adopted in nearly every state, represents a body of statutory law that governs important categories of contracts. The main articles that deal with the law of contracts are Article 1 (General Provisions) and Article 2 (Sales) . Sections of Article 9 (
perturbation.justification: The original text includes an integration clause which establishes that the written contract is the complete and final agreement between the parties. The changed text weakens this by stating it 'generally outlines the understanding' and 'may be subject to further clarifications.' This introduces ambiguity, potentially leading to disputes over the actual agreement. The Wex Law snippet states that a contract is 'an agreement between parties, creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law' and lists 'mutual assent' as a basic element of an enforceable contract. By making the agreement subject to further clarifications, the changed text undermines the principle of mutual assent and creates ambiguity, thus contradicting the need for clearly defined obligations and undermining the enforceability of the contract.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and all of the covenants, terms, provisions and conditions hereof shall be binding upon and inure to the benefit of the parties hereto and their respective heirs, legal representatives, successors and permitted assigns.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement should generally benefit the parties and might extend to their successors as appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: This clause specifies who is bound by and benefits from the agreement. The change weakens this, replacing 'shall be binding' with 'should generally benefit' and 'might extend to their successors as appropriate', making it less certain who the agreement applies to. This ambiguity undermines the clear legal obligations intended by the original clause.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law regarding assignment and succession of contracts.
perturbation.law_citation: Elf Atochem N. Am., Inc. v. Jaffari, 727 A.2d 286 (Del. 1999)
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware case law, such as Elf Atochem N. Am., Inc. v. Jaffari, 727 A.2d 286 (Del. 1999) emphasizes the importance of clearly defined terms regarding who is bound by a contract, including successors and assigns. The modified text, by weakening the binding nature of the agreement on successors and assigns, creates ambiguity and potential disputes over the agreement's scope, which is contrary to Delaware's emphasis on contractual certainty regarding these matters.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall be binding' to 'should generally benefit' and replacing definitive beneficiaries with 'might extend to their successors as appropriate' weakens the legally binding nature of the agreement. Although no scraped snippets are available, the perturbation explanation and law explanation point to Delaware law requiring clear definition of who is bound by a contract, especially successors and assigns. The modified text introduces ambiguity, potentially leading to disputes, which goes against the legal need for contractual certainty.
